narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a strong LSAT score and full-time enrollment plans, we understand how disappointing it must be to receive this rejection from our law school admissions process. Our model, trained on data from thousands of past students, predicted a 56% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which tipped the scales against admitting you at this time. This prediction stems from a careful look at key aspects of your academic record and background, compared to patterns seen in students who typically pass. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group—much lower than the average student's mid-range standing—and this stood out as the strongest influence on the model's concern. On a positive note, your LSAT score of 40, well above the typical 36.7 average, strongly pulled the prediction in the opposite direction, suggesting real potential there. Your Year 1 decile, also at the bottom compared to most students' averages around the middle, added further weight toward the failure risk, though less than Year 3. Meanwhile, your low family income bracket worked in your favor, as did your choice of full-time study, which aligns with the vast majority of successful students. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.20, below the average 3.21, had only a minor nudge toward failure. Overall, while your standout LSAT gives hope, the model saw your consistently low early deciles—far below what's typical for bar passers—as the dominant red flags, outweighing the positives in this holistic assessment. This isn't a judgment on your character or drive, but a data-driven signal based on how similar profiles have fared historically. To strengthen future applications, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school deciles, building on that impressive LSAT, and perhaps seeking mentorship tailored to students from low-income backgrounds like yours. Many applicants in your position have succeeded by addressing these gaps—keep pursuing your goals, and we wish you the best ahead.
